results.explanation: The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with write bandwidth close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The low CPU utilization and network usage further confirm that the bottleneck is not compute or network related but due to limited storage bandwidth. The workload is dominated by I/O time, consistent with a storage bandwidth saturation bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with write bandwidth close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The large average I/O size and low IOPS suggest efficient large sequential writes, consistent with bandwidth saturation rather than metadata or lock contention. Network utilization is low, and CPU is not heavily used, confirming storage bandwidth is the primary bottleneck.

The I/O bandwidth utilization is very high at 95% of peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The workload issues large sequential reads with a high average I/O size and moderate IOPS, consistent with bandwidth-bound performance. Network utilization is low, and CPU utilization is also low, confirming the bottleneck is at the storage bandwidth level.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 96% of peak capacity, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The workload issues large sequential writes with a high average IO size and moderate IOPS, consistent with bandwidth-bound behavior. Low CPU and network utilization further confirm that storage bandwidth is the primary bottleneck limiting I/O performance.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 96%, close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The I/O time dominates the stage execution (85%), while CPU and network utilization are low, confirming that storage bandwidth is the primary bottleneck limiting performance.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The workload is write-heavy with large sequential I/O and high aggregate data volume, causing the I/O subsystem to dominate execution time. Low CPU utilization confirms the bottleneck is not compute but storage bandwidth.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with write bandwidth close to the peak capacity of 150 MB/s. The I/O time ratio is also high, indicating the workflow is spending most of its time waiting on I/O. The average I/O size is large and sequential, which suggests efficient I/O patterns but the storage device itself is the limiting factor.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O but achieves very low bandwidth utilization with many small (4 KB) I/O operations and moderate IOPS, indicating many small file operations. Low CPU and network utilization exclude compute or network bottlenecks. The shared NFS storage with a single stripe and many small writes suggests metadata server contention is limiting performance.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O (84%) but the storage bandwidth utilization is very low (1.5%), indicating bandwidth is not saturated. The high IOPS with very small average I/O size (8 KB) and low sequential ratio suggest many small file operations stressing metadata servers. Low CPU and network utilization further support that metadata contention is the primary bottleneck.

The very low bandwidth utilization despite high I/O time and many small I/O operations (2 KB average size) indicates the workload is dominated by metadata overhead rather than data transfer. The high IOPS with tiny transfer sizes and low CPU and network utilization further support that metadata server contention is the primary bottleneck.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O but bandwidth utilization is very low, indicating the storage device is underutilized. The high number of operations with small average I/O size and low sequential ratio suggests many small, random reads causing metadata server contention. CPU utilization is low, so compute is not the bottleneck, and network is not saturated. This pattern is typical of metadata contention from many small file operations.

The workflow shows very low bandwidth utilization despite high I/O time and many small I/O operations (4 KB average size) with a high number of operations (333,000) and many parallel tasks (80). This pattern indicates the metadata server is overwhelmed by numerous small file reads, causing metadata contention and limiting effective throughput.

The very low bandwidth utilization despite high I/O time and a large number of small I/O operations (16 KB) indicates the system is overwhelmed by metadata operations rather than data transfer. The moderate IOPS with tiny average I/O size and low CPU utilization further support that many small writes are causing metadata server contention, limiting effective throughput.

The workflow shows very high I/O time ratio with extremely low bandwidth utilization and very small average I/O size, indicating many small writes. The high IOPS combined with tiny transfer sizes and low CPU utilization suggests the metadata server is overwhelmed by numerous small file operations, causing metadata contention rather than bandwidth or compute limits.

Despite high parallelism (200 tasks) and a large aggregate write size, the write bandwidth utilization is low (21% of peak) with many writers (200) contending on a single shared output file. This suggests multiple tasks are competing for write locks, causing serialization and low effective bandwidth. The high IO time ratio confirms I/O is the bottleneck, but bandwidth and network are underutilized, pointing to lock contention as the primary issue.

Despite many tasks writing in parallel with large IO sizes, the bandwidth utilization is low and the IO time ratio is very high, indicating inefficient IO progress. The presence of only two shared output files with 64 writers each suggests heavy lock contention on these shared files, limiting effective write throughput and causing serialization at the file lock level.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many concurrent writers (300) on a single shared output file, the bandwidth utilization is very low (19% of peak), indicating contention on write locks. The low CPU utilization and moderate IOPS with relatively large IO size exclude compute or metadata contention. This pattern is typical of lock contention limiting parallel write throughput.

Despite many writers (64) writing to a single shared file, the bandwidth utilization is low (0.21) while I/O time dominates execution (82%). This indicates multiple tasks are contending for write locks on the shared file, limiting effective parallel I/O bandwidth and causing serialization at the lock level rather than at the I/O or network level.

Despite many tasks writing in parallel with large IO sizes and low storage bandwidth utilization, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak capacity. The presence of only 4 shared output files with 64 writers each suggests heavy lock contention on these shared files, limiting effective parallel write throughput and causing the I/O bottleneck.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many parallel tasks, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak storage bandwidth, indicating inefficient I/O. The presence of only two shared output files with 200 writers each suggests heavy lock contention on shared files, limiting effective write throughput. Low CPU utilization further supports that the bottleneck is in I/O coordination rather than compute or network.

Despite high parallelism and many writers per shared file (167 writers on only 3 shared files), the write bandwidth utilization is low (0.22) and CPU utilization is low, indicating I/O is active but throughput is limited. This pattern strongly suggests multiple tasks are contending for write locks on a small number of shared files, causing serialization and low effective bandwidth.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1) compared to the network utilization ratio (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workflow reads from S3, a remote object store, and the network bandwidth is nearly saturated while storage bandwidth is underutilized. High I/O time ratio with low CPU utilization further supports that data transfer over the network is the primary bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1) while the network utilization is very high (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor in data transfer. The workload is read-heavy with large IO sizes and moderate CPU usage, so the bottleneck is the network bandwidth saturating during data fetch from the NFS storage.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) compared to the network utilization ratio (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workflow reads a large dataset from S3, a remote object store, causing the network to saturate while storage bandwidth remains underutilized. High I/O time ratio and low CPU utilization further support that data transfer over the network is the primary bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) while the network utilization is very high (0.94), indicating that the network is the limiting factor in data transfer. The workflow reads from an S3 object store, which relies heavily on network throughput. The high network utilization combined with low CPU and storage bandwidth usage confirms a network I/O bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) compared to the peak capacity, indicating storage is not saturated. However, the network utilization ratio is very high (0.94), showing the network is nearly fully utilized. Since the storage is S3 (object storage) and the workflow involves distributed training with many tasks, the network is the primary bottleneck limiting I/O throughput.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1) compared to the network utilization which is near saturation (0.95). This indicates that the network is the limiting factor in data movement, preventing the storage from being fully utilized. The high I/O time ratio further supports that I/O is the bottleneck, but the bottleneck lies in network throughput rather than storage or compute.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) compared to the network utilization ratio (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workflow reads from remote S3 storage, and the network bandwidth is nearly saturated, causing high I/O time despite ample storage bandwidth capacity.

Despite many tasks running in parallel, I/O is serialized through a single writer/reader, limiting bandwidth utilization to 12% of peak and causing a high I/O time ratio. Low CPU utilization indicates the bottleneck is not compute, and network and storage bandwidth are underutilized, confirming serialization is the primary constraint.

The I/O is forced through a single writer/reader as indicated by io_parallelism=1 and io_workers=1, severely limiting bandwidth utilization despite a large amount of data and high I/O time ratio. Low CPU utilization and low bandwidth usage relative to peak storage bandwidth confirm that the bottleneck is due to serialization of I/O rather than storage or network saturation.

Despite many tasks and nodes, I/O parallelism is limited to a single writer, severely restricting throughput and causing high I/O time ratio. The low bandwidth utilization relative to peak storage bandwidth indicates the storage is underutilized due to serialization. This serialization prevents the workflow from leveraging available storage and network resources effectively.

The I/O is highly serialized with only one parallel I/O worker and io_parallelism=1, severely limiting bandwidth utilization despite a large aggregate data size and long I/O time. Low CPU utilization and low storage bandwidth usage indicate the bottleneck is due to lack of parallel I/O rather than storage or network saturation.

Despite many tasks and high I/O time ratio, the bandwidth utilization is low and io_parallelism is 1, indicating all I/O is serialized through a single writer. This serialization prevents parallel I/O throughput, causing the workflow to be I/O bound but underutilizing available storage bandwidth and network.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many tasks, the I/O parallelism is limited to a single writer/reader, severely restricting throughput. This serialization prevents effective utilization of available storage bandwidth and network capacity, causing the low bandwidth utilization and high I/O wait time.

Despite many tasks and nodes, I/O parallelism is limited to a single worker, causing serialization of I/O operations. This prevents the workflow from utilizing available storage bandwidth and network capacity, resulting in low bandwidth utilization and high I/O time ratio. CPU utilization is low, indicating the bottleneck is in I/O serialization rather than compute.

The workflow spends over half its time in I/O, dominated by frequent large checkpoint writes (8000 MB every 300s, 12 times total). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization (0.12) and low CPU usage, the large and frequent checkpointing operations cause significant overhead, limiting overall progress. Other bottlenecks like bandwidth saturation or network limits are not supported by the metrics.

The workflow spends a large portion of time on I/O (58%) dominated by frequent, large checkpoint writes (15 checkpoints of 15 GB each). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage, the periodic checkpointing overhead significantly impacts overall runtime, indicating checkpointing is the primary bottleneck.

The workflow spends half its time on I/O, dominated by large, frequent checkpoint writes totaling 180,000 MB over 6 intervals. Despite low storage bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage, the periodic large checkpointing operations cause significant overhead, limiting overall progress.

The workflow spends a large portion of time on I/O (44%) with moderate bandwidth utilization, indicating I/O is significant but not saturated. The very large checkpoint size (8 GB) and frequent checkpointing (every 120s, 30 times) result in substantial total checkpoint I/O matching the aggregate data size. Low CPU utilization suggests the system is waiting on I/O related to checkpointing rather than compute or network limits.

The workflow spends over half its time on I/O dominated by large, frequent checkpoint writes (8000 MB every 300 seconds, 12 times total). CPU utilization is low, indicating compute is not the bottleneck. The storage bandwidth utilization is low, so the overhead is due to the checkpointing frequency and size rather than device saturation or contention.

The workflow spends a significant portion of time (40%) on I/O, with very large and frequent checkpoints totaling 800 GB over the run. This checkpointing overhead dominates the I/O time, limiting overall progress despite moderate storage bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage.

The workflow spends 75% of its time on I/O dominated by frequent large checkpoint writes (8 GB every 180 seconds, 20 times total). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization (0.15) and low CPU usage (18%), the high checkpointing frequency and size cause significant overhead, limiting overall progress. Other bottlenecks like bandwidth saturation or metadata contention are not supported by the metrics.

The CPU utilization is very high at 92%, while I/O time is only 9% of total time and storage bandwidth utilization is low at 16%. Network usage is negligible, and I/O parallelism is high with large sequential reads. These indicate that the workflow is limited by compute rather than I/O or network.

The CPU utilization is very high at 88%, while I/O time ratio is low at 12%, indicating that the workflow spends most of its time computing rather than waiting on I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization is only 20%, and network utilization is negligible, so neither storage nor network is a bottleneck. This points to compute being the primary bottleneck.

The CPU utilization is very high at 85%, while I/O time is only 10% of total time and storage bandwidth utilization is low (13%). Network utilization is negligible, and I/O operations are relatively large and sequential, indicating no metadata or lock contention. This shows the workflow is primarily limited by compute rather than I/O.

The CPU utilization is very high at 94%, while I/O time is only 6% of total time and storage bandwidth utilization is very low at 8%. Network usage is negligible, indicating that the workflow is limited by computation rather than I/O or network resources.

The CPU utilization is very high at 96%, while I/O time is only 4% of total time, indicating that computation dominates the stage runtime. Storage bandwidth and network utilization are low, so I/O is not limiting performance. This points to a compute-bound bottleneck.

The CPU utilization is very high at 90%, while I/O time ratio is low at 9%, indicating that the workflow is spending most of its time computing rather than waiting on I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization and network utilization are both very low, ruling out I/O or network bottlenecks. Therefore, the primary bottleneck is compute bound.

The CPU utilization is very high at 82%, while I/O time ratio is low at 18%, indicating that the workflow is spending most of its time computing rather than waiting on I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization is low (23%) and network usage is negligible, so neither storage nor network are bottlenecks. This points to compute being the primary bottleneck.

The CPU utilization is very high at 87%, while I/O time ratio is low at 11%, indicating that the workload is primarily limited by computation rather than I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization is low (22%) and network utilization is minimal (1%), so neither storage nor network are bottlenecks. The data size and I/O patterns do not suggest metadata or lock contention.
